resComment: Reduced hemoglobin indicates anemia, which may arise from iron deficiency, chronic disease, renal insufficiency, or occult blood loss.
Hemoglobin concentration is within the expected reference range, indicating adequate oxygen-carrying capacity.
Elevated hemoglobin suggests polycythemia vera, chronic hypoxia, or dehydration.
Leukopenia may result from bone marrow suppression, viral infections, autoimmune disease, or cytotoxic medications.
WBC count is within normal limits, indicating no evidence of acute infection or marrow suppression.
Leukocytosis suggests infection, inflammation, stress response, or hematological malignancy.
Thrombocytopenia increases bleeding risk and may result from bone marrow suppression, immune thrombocytopenia, infections, or drug-induced causes.
Platelet count is within normal limits, indicating adequate hemostatic function.
Thrombocytosis may be reactive (infection, inflammation, iron deficiency) or due to myeloproliferative disorders.
Neutrophilia suggests bacterial infection, inflammation, stress, or corticosteroid therapy.
Lymphocytosis is commonly seen in viral infections, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, or autoimmune conditions.
Eosinophilia suggests allergic reactions, parasitic infections, or hypereosinophilic syndromes.
Basophilia may occur in myeloproliferative disorders, chronic inflammation, or hypersensitivity reactions.
Monocytosis suggests chronic infections (e.g., tuberculosis), autoimmune disease, or hematological malignancy.
Elevated reticulocyte count indicates increased red cell production, commonly seen in hemolysis or recovery from anemia.
Low reticulocyte count suggests impaired bone marrow response, as seen in aplastic anemia or marrow suppression.
Abnormal peripheral smear may reveal anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, sickle cells, blasts, or parasites.
Peripheral smear shows normal red cell, white cell, and platelet morphology.
Elevated ESR indicates inflammation, infection, autoimmune disease, or malignancy.
Abnormal CBC may reveal anemia, leukocytosis, leukopenia, or thrombocytopenia.
Abnormal FBC indicates hematological abnormalities such as anemia, infection, or bone marrow suppression.
Low hemoglobin indicates anemia. Sahli’s method provides approximate values.
Elevated hemoglobin suggests polycythemia vera, chronic hypoxia, or dehydration.
Detection of HbC variant indicates hemoglobin C trait or disease.
Abnormal RFT may reveal elevated creatinine, BUN, or electrolyte imbalance. Findings suggest impaired renal function. Correlation with eGFR and urinalysis is required.
Abnormal LFT may reveal elevated ALT, AST, ALP, or bilirubin. Findings suggest hepatocellular injury, cholestasis, or impaired synthetic function. Clinical correlation and imaging are recommended.
Abnormal film comment may reveal anisocytosis, poikilocytosis, sickle cells, blasts, or parasites.
Peripheral smear shows normal red cell, white cell, and platelet morphology.
Elevated fasting glucose suggests impaired fasting glucose or diabetes mellitus. Correlation with HbA1c and oral glucose tolerance test is recommended.
Low fasting glucose may indicate hypoglycemia due to insulin therapy, fasting, or endocrine disorders.
Elevated random blood sugar suggests diabetes mellitus. Correlation with fasting glucose and HbA1c is required.
Elevated HbA1c indicates poor glycemic control over the past 2–3 months. Values ≥6.5% are diagnostic of diabetes mellitus.
Elevated creatinine indicates impaired renal function. Correlation with eGFR is required.
Elevated BUN suggests renal impairment, dehydration, or high protein catabolism.
Elevated uric acid suggests gout, renal impairment, or increased cell turnover.
Hypernatremia suggests dehydration, excessive sodium intake, or endocrine disorders.
Hyponatremia may result from fluid overload, SIADH, renal disease, or diuretic use.
Hyperkalemia may result from renal failure, hemolysis, or potassium-sparing drugs. It can cause cardiac arrhythmias.
Hypokalemia may result from diuretic use, vomiting, diarrhea, or endocrine disorders. It can cause muscle weakness and arrhythmias.
Hyperchloremia may result from dehydration, renal tubular acidosis, or excessive saline infusion.
Hypochloremia may result from vomiting, metabolic alkalosis, or diuretic use.
Elevated bicarbonate suggests metabolic alkalosis or compensation for respiratory acidosis.
Low bicarbonate suggests metabolic acidosis or compensation for respiratory alkalosis.
Elevated ALT suggests hepatocellular injury, commonly due to viral hepatitis, fatty liver disease, or drug-induced liver injury.
Elevated AST suggests hepatocellular injury, muscle damage, or alcohol-related liver disease.
Elevated ALP suggests cholestasis, biliary obstruction, bone disease, or infiltrative liver disorders.
Elevated bilirubin suggests hepatocellular dysfunction, hemolysis, or biliary obstruction.
Low albumin suggests chronic liver disease, nephrotic syndrome, or malnutrition.
Low total protein suggests malnutrition, liver disease, or protein-losing enteropathy.
Elevated total protein may indicate chronic inflammation, infections, or plasma cell disorders such as multiple myeloma.
Elevated cholesterol increases cardiovascular risk. Correlation with LDL and HDL levels is required.
Elevated triglycerides increase risk of pancreatitis and cardiovascular disease. Often associated with metabolic syndrome.
Low HDL increases cardiovascular risk. Lifestyle modification and exercise are recommended.
Elevated LDL is a major risk factor for atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease.
Elevated amylase suggests acute pancreatitis, salivary gland disease, or renal impairment.
Elevated lipase is more specific for acute pancreatitis and pancreatic injury.
Elevated LDH suggests tissue breakdown, hemolysis, myocardial infarction, or malignancy.
Elevated CK suggests muscle injury, myocardial infarction, or rhabdomyolysis.
Elevated TSH suggests primary hypothyroidism or inadequate thyroid hormone replacement.
Low TSH suggests hyperthyroidism or excessive thyroid hormone replacement.
Elevated T3 suggests hyperthyroidism or thyrotoxicosis.
Low T3 suggests hypothyroidism or non-thyroidal illness.
Elevated T4 suggests hyperthyroidism or thyrotoxicosis.
Low T4 suggests hypothyroidism or impaired thyroid hormone production.
Elevated cortisol suggests Cushing’s syndrome, stress, or corticosteroid therapy.
Low cortisol suggests adrenal insufficiency (Addison’s disease) or pituitary dysfunction.
Hypercalcemia suggests hyperparathyroidism, malignancy, or vitamin D intoxication.
Hypocalcemia suggests hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, or chronic kidney disease.
Hyperphosphatemia suggests renal failure, hypoparathyroidism, or tumor lysis syndrome.
Hypophosphatemia suggests malnutrition, hyperparathyroidism, or chronic alcohol use.
Hypermagnesemia suggests renal failure or excessive magnesium intake. May cause neuromuscular depression.
Hypomagnesemia suggests malnutrition, chronic alcoholism, or diuretic use. May cause arrhythmias and neuromuscular irritability.
Low serum iron suggests iron deficiency anemia or chronic disease.
Elevated serum iron suggests hemochromatosis, hemolysis, or iron overload.
Low ferritin indicates iron deficiency anemia.
Elevated ferritin suggests iron overload, inflammation, or liver disease.
Elevated CRP indicates acute inflammation, infection, or tissue injury. It is a nonspecific marker.
Elevated hs-CRP suggests increased cardiovascular risk due to chronic low-grade inflammation.
Elevated troponin indicates myocardial injury, commonly due to acute myocardial infarction.
Elevated BNP suggests heart failure and increased ventricular wall stress.
Elevated procalcitonin suggests bacterial infection or sepsis. Useful for guiding antibiotic therapy.
Low vitamin D suggests deficiency, associated with bone weakness, rickets, or osteoporosis.
Elevated vitamin D suggests excessive supplementation and risk of hypercalcemia.
Low vitamin B12 suggests deficiency, associated with megaloblastic anemia and neuropathy.
Elevated vitamin B12 may result from supplementation or liver disease.
Low folate suggests deficiency, associated with megaloblastic anemia.
Elevated homocysteine suggests increased cardiovascular risk and may indicate folate or B12 deficiency.
Positive blood culture indicates bacteremia or septicemia. Organism identification and sensitivity testing are required.
Positive stool culture indicates enteric infection such as Salmonella, Shigella, or pathogenic E. coli.
Positive sputum culture indicates respiratory infection such as pneumonia, tuberculosis, or bronchitis. Correlation with clinical findings is required.
Gram-positive or Gram-negative organisms detected. Useful for guiding initial antibiotic therapy.
Acid-fast bacilli detected, suggestive of Mycobacterium tuberculosis or related species.
Fungal hyphae or yeast cells detected, indicating superficial or systemic fungal infection.
Encapsulated yeast detected, suggestive of Cryptococcus species.
Elevated Widal titers suggest Salmonella infection (typhoid fever). Requires correlation with clinical findings.
Reactive VDRL suggests syphilis infection. Confirmation with treponemal-specific tests is required.
Reactive HIV test suggests HIV infection. Confirmation with Western blot or PCR is required.
Positive HBsAg indicates active Hepatitis B infection. Requires further serological profiling.
Positive Anti-HCV antibody suggests Hepatitis C infection. Confirmation with HCV RNA testing is required.
Presence of ova or parasites indicates intestinal parasitic infection such as Giardia, Entamoeba histolytica, or helminths.
Positive smear indicates malaria infection. Species identification and parasite density are required.
Positive rapid test indicates malaria infection. Confirmation with microscopy is recommended.
Positive occult blood suggests gastrointestinal bleeding, peptic ulcer disease, or colorectal cancer.
Positive fungal culture indicates systemic or localized fungal infection. Species identification is required.
Positive Candida culture suggests candidiasis, which may be superficial or systemic depending on clinical context.
Positive Aspergillus antigen test suggests invasive aspergillosis. Correlation with imaging and culture is required.
Positive Cryptococcus antigen test suggests cryptococcal infection, often associated with immunocompromised states.
Positive ANA suggests autoimmune disease such as systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren’s syndrome, or mixed connective tissue disease.
Elevated RF suggests rheumatoid arthritis or other autoimmune conditions. Correlation with anti-CCP antibody is recommended.
Positive anti-dsDNA antibody is highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus.
Positive anti-CCP antibody suggests rheumatoid arthritis with higher specificity than RF.
Elevated CRP indicates systemic inflammation, autoimmune activity, or infection. Used as a marker of disease activity.
Low C3 suggests complement consumption in autoimmune disease (e.g., lupus) or recurrent infections.
Low C4 suggests complement consumption in autoimmune disease, especially systemic lupus erythematosus.
Elevated IgG suggests chronic infection, autoimmune disease, or multiple myeloma.
Elevated IgA suggests chronic liver disease, autoimmune disease, or IgA nephropathy.
Elevated IgM suggests acute infection or Waldenström’s macroglobulinemia.
Elevated IgE suggests allergic disease, parasitic infection, or atopic conditions.
Positive Anti-Smith antibody is highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
Positive Anti-RNP antibody suggests mixed connective tissue disease or overlap syndromes.
Positive Anti-centromere antibody is associated with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (CREST syndrome).
Positive Anti-mitochondrial antibody is strongly associated with primary biliary cholangitis.
Positive Anti-Scl-70 antibody is associated with diffuse systemic sclerosis and interstitial lung disease.
Histological examination reveals abnormal or malignant cells. Diagnosis depends on tissue type and pathology findings.
Presence of malignant or atypical cells suggests neoplastic disease. Further histological confirmation may be required.

normalRange: Negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy
resComment: Abnormal Pap smear suggests cervical dysplasia or malignancy. Requires correlation with HPV testing and colposcopy.
Abnormal findings suggest hematological disorders such as leukemia, lymphoma, or aplastic anemia.
Abnormal H&E findings reveal tissue pathology such as inflammation, dysplasia, or malignancy.
Positive PAS stain indicates glycogen storage, fungal organisms, or basement membrane thickening.
Positive cytokeratin staining indicates epithelial origin of tumor cells.
Positive CD3/CD20 staining helps differentiate T-cell vs B-cell lymphomas.
Positive HER2/neu staining indicates HER2-positive breast cancer, guiding targeted therapy.
Abnormal karyotype suggests chromosomal disorders such as Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, or translocations in malignancies.
Positive FISH indicates specific chromosomal abnormalities, useful in leukemia, lymphoma, and solid tumors.
Positive PCR indicates presence of specific genetic material, useful for infectious disease diagnosis and mutation analysis.
NGS detects genetic mutations, insertions, deletions, or rearrangements. Useful in oncology and inherited disease profiling.
Abnormal Southern blot indicates gene rearrangements or mutations, historically used in genetic disease diagnosis.
Prolonged PT suggests clotting factor deficiency, liver disease, vitamin K deficiency, or anticoagulant therapy (e.g., warfarin).
Elevated INR indicates increased bleeding risk, commonly due to anticoagulant therapy or liver dysfunction.
Prolonged aPTT suggests intrinsic pathway abnormalities, hemophilia, or heparin therapy.
Prolonged TT suggests fibrinogen abnormalities, dysfibrinogenemia, or anticoagulant therapy.
Elevated D-dimer suggests active fibrinolysis, seen in deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, or disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC).
Determines the ABO blood group system. Critical for transfusion compatibility.
Determines Rh status. Important for transfusion and pregnancy management.
Positive result indicates immune-mediated hemolysis, such as autoimmune hemolytic anemia or hemolytic disease of the newborn.
Positive result indicates presence of antibodies against red blood cells. Used in crossmatching and prenatal testing.
Incompatible crossmatch indicates risk of transfusion reaction. Essential before blood transfusion.
Prolonged bleeding time suggests platelet dysfunction, von Willebrand disease, or thrombocytopenia.
Prolonged clotting time suggests coagulation factor deficiency or anticoagulant therapy.
Failure to correct prolonged PT/aPTT indicates presence of inhibitors (e.g., lupus anticoagulant). Correction suggests factor deficiency.
Low Factor VIII activity suggests Hemophilia A or acquired deficiency.
Low Factor IX activity suggests Hemophilia B (Christmas disease).
Low fibrinogen suggests disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), liver disease, or congenital afibrinogenemia.
Positive thrombophilia panel indicates inherited or acquired clotting disorders (e.g., Factor V Leiden, Protein C/S deficiency, Antithrombin deficiency).
Elevated fasting insulin suggests insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome, or early type 2 diabetes.
Elevated GH suggests acromegaly or gigantism. Suppression testing with glucose is required.
Elevated prolactin suggests prolactinoma, hypothyroidism, or drug-induced hyperprolactinemia.
Low testosterone suggests hypogonadism, pituitary dysfunction, or chronic illness.
Abnormal estradiol levels suggest ovarian dysfunction, menopause, or estrogen-secreting tumors.
Abnormal progesterone levels suggest luteal phase defect, pregnancy status, or ovarian dysfunction.
Elevated ACTH suggests Cushing’s disease (pituitary cause) or ectopic ACTH production.
Elevated aldosterone suggests primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn’s syndrome).
Elevated renin activity suggests secondary hyperaldosteronism due to renal artery stenosis or diuretic use.
Elevated DHEA-S suggests adrenal hyperplasia, adrenal tumors, or polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).
Elevated 17-hydroxyprogesterone suggests congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH).
Positive adrenal antibodies suggest autoimmune adrenalitis, a common cause of Addison’s disease.
Elevated PSA suggests prostate cancer, benign prostatic hyperplasia, or prostatitis. Requires correlation with clinical findings.
Elevated CEA suggests colorectal cancer, gastric cancer, or pancreatic cancer. Also elevated in smokers.
Elevated AFP suggests hepatocellular carcinoma, germ cell tumors, or liver disease.
Elevated CA-125 suggests ovarian cancer, but may also be elevated in endometriosis or pelvic inflammatory disease.
Elevated CA 19-9 suggests pancreatic cancer, cholangiocarcinoma, or gastrointestinal malignancy.
Elevated beta-hCG suggests germ cell tumors, trophoblastic disease, or testicular cancer.
Pathogenic BRCA1/2 mutations increase risk of breast and ovarian cancer. Genetic counseling is recommended.
EGFR mutations are associated with non-small cell lung cancer and guide targeted therapy.
KRAS mutations are common in colorectal and pancreatic cancers. They predict resistance to certain targeted therapies.
Positive BCR-ABL fusion gene confirms chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). Guides tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy.
HER2 gene amplification is associated with aggressive breast cancer. Guides trastuzumab therapy.
TP53 mutations are linked to multiple cancers and Li-Fraumeni syndrome. Indicates poor prognosis in many malignancies.
Positive TB PCR confirms Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection and detects rifampicin resistance.
Positive high-risk HPV genotype (e.g., 16, 18) indicates increased risk for cervical cancer.
Positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR confirms COVID-19 infection. Cycle threshold values may indicate viral load.
Elevated HIV viral load indicates active replication. Used to monitor treatment response.
Detectable HCV RNA confirms active Hepatitis C infection. Guides antiviral therapy.
Positive malaria PCR confirms Plasmodium infection and allows species identification.
Low carnitine suggests primary carnitine deficiency or secondary deficiency due to metabolic disorders.
Abnormal amino acid profile suggests inborn errors of metabolism such as phenylketonuria or maple syrup urine disease.
Abnormal organic acids indicate metabolic disorders such as mitochondrial disease or organic acidemias.
Low zinc suggests nutritional deficiency, chronic illness, or malabsorption.
Low copper suggests Wilson’s disease or malnutrition. Elevated copper may occur in inflammation or liver disease.
Low selenium suggests deficiency, associated with cardiomyopathy (Keshan disease) or impaired immunity.
Low urinary iodine suggests iodine deficiency, leading to goiter or hypothyroidism.
Abnormal ECG may reveal arrhythmias, ischemia, myocardial infarction, or conduction defects.
Abnormal echocardiogram may reveal valvular disease, cardiomyopathy, or reduced ejection fraction.
Elevated troponin T indicates myocardial injury, commonly due to acute coronary syndrome.
Elevated NT-proBNP suggests heart failure and increased ventricular wall stress.
Elevated Lp(a) is an independent risk factor for atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease.
Elevated calcium score indicates coronary artery calcification and increased risk of coronary artery disease.
Abnormal spirometry indicates obstructive or restrictive lung disease. Common in asthma, COPD, or interstitial lung disease.
Low PEFR suggests airway obstruction, commonly seen in asthma exacerbations.
Abnormal ABG indicates respiratory or metabolic acidosis/alkalosis, hypoxemia, or hypercapnia.

testName: Diffusing Capacity of the Lung for Carbon Monoxide (DLCO)
resComment: Low DLCO suggests impaired gas exchange, seen in interstitial lung disease, emphysema, or pulmonary hypertension.
Abnormal chest X-ray may reveal pneumonia, tuberculosis, lung masses, or pleural effusion.
Abnormal HRCT reveals interstitial lung disease, pulmonary fibrosis, or emphysema.

normalRange: Clear, no protein, no blood, no glucose
resComment: Abnormal urinalysis may reveal proteinuria, hematuria, glycosuria, or infection.
Elevated urine protein suggests nephrotic syndrome, glomerulonephritis, or diabetic nephropathy.
Low creatinine clearance indicates impaired renal function. Used to estimate GFR.
Low eGFR indicates chronic kidney disease. Staging depends on severity of reduction.
Elevated microalbuminuria suggests early diabetic nephropathy or hypertensive renal damage.
Abnormal urine electrolytes help differentiate prerenal vs intrinsic renal failure and assess tubular function.
Abnormal EEG may reveal epilepsy, encephalopathy, or focal brain lesions.
Abnormal EMG suggests neuromuscular disorders such as myopathy or peripheral neuropathy.
Abnormal NCS indicates peripheral neuropathy, demyelination, or axonal damage.
Abnormal CSF may reveal meningitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage, or multiple sclerosis.
Abnormal MRI may reveal stroke, tumors, demyelination, or neurodegenerative changes.
Abnormal evoked potentials suggest demyelinating diseases such as multiple sclerosis or sensory pathway dysfunction.
Positive ANA suggests autoimmune disease such as systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren’s syndrome, or scleroderma.
Elevated RF suggests rheumatoid arthritis or other autoimmune conditions.
Positive anti-dsDNA antibody is highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).
Positive anti-CCP antibody strongly suggests rheumatoid arthritis and predicts disease severity.
Low complement levels suggest active autoimmune disease such as lupus or immune complex-mediated disorders.
Abnormal immunoglobulin levels suggest immune deficiency, chronic infection, allergy, or autoimmune disease.
Elevated PD-L1 expression suggests potential benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors in cancers such as NSCLC and melanoma.
Positive CTLA-4 expression indicates immune checkpoint pathway activation. Guides use of CTLA-4 inhibitors in immunotherapy.
Abnormal cytokine levels suggest immune dysregulation, cytokine storm, or tumor‑associated inflammation.
Abnormal immunophenotyping reveals hematological malignancies (e.g., leukemia, lymphoma) or immune deficiency.
High TILs indicate active immune response against tumor. Associated with better prognosis in breast and melanoma cancers.
Pathogenic variants in immune checkpoint genes may influence response to immunotherapy and cancer progression.
Abnormal bone marrow findings may reveal leukemia, lymphoma, aplastic anemia, or myelodysplastic syndromes.
Abnormal blast immunophenotyping confirms acute leukemia subtype and guides treatment.
Abnormal karyotype reveals chromosomal translocations or deletions, important in leukemia prognosis.
Positive FISH detects genetic abnormalities such as BCR-ABL, PML-RARA, or del(17p).
Positive MRD indicates persistence of leukemic cells after treatment. Critical for prognosis and therapy adjustment.
Positive JAK2 mutation suggests myeloproliferative neoplasms such as polycythemia vera or essential thrombocythemia.
Abnormal biopsy histology may reveal malignancy, chronic inflammation, or degenerative changes.

normalRange: Negative for intraepithelial lesion or malignancy
resComment: Abnormal Pap smear suggests cervical dysplasia, HPV infection, or carcinoma in situ.
Abnormal FNAC findings may reveal neoplastic cells, granulomatous inflammation, or metastatic disease.
Positive IHC markers (e.g., HER2, ER, PR, Ki-67) guide cancer diagnosis, prognosis, and targeted therapy.
Abnormal frozen section confirms intraoperative diagnosis of malignancy or tissue pathology.
Abnormal cytogenetic cytology reveals chromosomal abnormalities associated with hematological malignancies.
Elevated digoxin levels suggest toxicity, presenting with arrhythmias, nausea, or visual disturbances.
Elevated lithium levels indicate toxicity, with neurological and renal complications.
Elevated valproic acid levels suggest toxicity, with hepatotoxicity and CNS depression.
Elevated lead levels indicate lead poisoning, associated with anemia, neuropathy, and cognitive impairment.
Elevated mercury levels suggest exposure, causing neurotoxicity and renal impairment.
Elevated arsenic levels indicate poisoning, presenting with GI symptoms, neuropathy, and skin changes.
Positive urine drug screen indicates presence of substances such as opioids, cannabinoids, amphetamines, or benzodiazepines.
Pathogenic variants detected in exome sequencing may explain inherited disorders or cancer predisposition.
Pathogenic variants detected in genome sequencing provide comprehensive insight into genetic disease risk.
Abnormal pharmacogenomic variants affect drug metabolism and guide personalized therapy.
Pathogenic mitochondrial DNA mutations cause mitochondrial disorders with neuromuscular and metabolic manifestations.
Abnormal HLA typing is associated with autoimmune diseases and guides organ transplantation compatibility.
High PRS indicates increased genetic susceptibility to complex diseases such as diabetes, heart disease, or cancer.
Pathogen genome sequencing identifies species, virulence factors, and resistance genes for precision treatment.
Positive mecA gene confirms methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Guides antibiotic selection.
Detection of rpoB, katG, or inhA mutations indicates resistance to rifampicin and isoniazid in TB.
Positive carbapenemase genes indicate carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE). Critical for infection control.
Detection of HIV resistance mutations guides antiretroviral therapy selection.
Sequencing influenza virus identifies strain type and mutations relevant for vaccine design and antiviral resistance.
Elevated GH suggests acromegaly or gigantism. Suppression testing with glucose is recommended.
Elevated insulin suggests insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome, or insulinoma.
Elevated IGF-1 supports diagnosis of acromegaly. Low levels suggest GH deficiency.
Elevated prolactin suggests prolactinoma, hypothyroidism, or drug-induced hyperprolactinemia.
Abnormal leptin levels indicate obesity, metabolic syndrome, or lipodystrophy.
Low adiponectin suggests insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular risk.
Abnormal FSH suggests ovarian failure, menopause, or testicular dysfunction.
Abnormal LH indicates ovulatory dysfunction, PCOS, or pituitary disorders.
Abnormal estradiol suggests ovarian dysfunction, estrogen-secreting tumors, or menopausal status.
Low luteal progesterone suggests anovulation or luteal phase defect.
Abnormal testosterone suggests hypogonadism, PCOS, or androgen-secreting tumors.
Low AMH indicates reduced ovarian reserve. Elevated AMH may suggest PCOS.
Abnormal semen analysis suggests male infertility due to low count, poor motility, or abnormal morphology.
Low APGAR score indicates neonatal distress, requiring immediate resuscitation or supportive care.
Elevated neonatal bilirubin suggests jaundice, hemolysis, or impaired hepatic conjugation.
Positive PKU screening indicates phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency. Requires dietary management.
Elevated TSH in newborn suggests congenital hypothyroidism. Early treatment prevents developmental delay.
Positive CF screening suggests cystic fibrosis. Confirmed by sweat chloride testing and genetic analysis.
Abnormal growth chart indicates undernutrition, endocrine disorders, or chronic illness.
Low bone density indicates osteopenia or osteoporosis, increasing fracture risk.
High frailty index suggests vulnerability to adverse health outcomes and reduced resilience.
Low vitamin D is common in elderly patients and increases risk of falls, fractures, and bone disease.
Low MMSE score suggests cognitive impairment, dementia, or delirium.
High fall risk suggests impaired balance, muscle weakness, or environmental hazards.
Abnormal CGA reveals deficits in physical, cognitive, psychological, or social domains requiring multidisciplinary care.
Elevated lactate indicates tissue hypoperfusion, sepsis, or shock. Used to monitor resuscitation effectiveness.
Abnormal ABG indicates respiratory failure, metabolic acidosis/alkalosis, or impaired oxygenation.
Abnormal shock panel suggests circulatory collapse, trauma, or sepsis.
Low GCS indicates impaired consciousness due to trauma, stroke, or intoxication.
Elevated procalcitonin suggests bacterial sepsis. Guides antibiotic therapy decisions.
Elevated D-dimer suggests thromboembolism, disseminated intravascular coagulation, or trauma.
Elevated blood lead indicates occupational exposure, associated with anemia, neuropathy, and cognitive impairment.
Positive pesticide biomarkers suggest agricultural or industrial exposure. Associated with neurotoxicity and endocrine disruption.
Elevated carboxyhemoglobin indicates carbon monoxide poisoning, common in fire or industrial exposure.
Abnormal audiometry suggests occupational hearing loss due to chronic noise exposure.

testName: Air Quality Impact Test (Particulate Matter PM2.5)
resComment: Elevated PM2.5 exposure increases risk of respiratory and cardiovascular disease.
Elevated cadmium suggests occupational or environmental exposure, associated with renal and bone toxicity.
Abnormal VO₂ max indicates reduced aerobic capacity or exceptional endurance performance.
Abnormal lactate threshold suggests impaired endurance or elite adaptation to high-intensity exercise.
Elevated CK indicates muscle injury, overtraining, or rhabdomyolysis.
Abnormal urine specific gravity suggests dehydration or overhydration.
Elevated myoglobin indicates acute muscle injury or rhabdomyolysis.
Abnormal body composition indicates undernutrition, obesity, or elite athletic conditioning.
Low vitamin A suggests deficiency, associated with night blindness and immune dysfunction.
Low vitamin E suggests deficiency, associated with neuropathy and hemolysis.
Low zinc suggests deficiency, associated with poor wound healing, growth retardation, and immune dysfunction.
Abnormal copper levels suggest Wilson’s disease, Menkes disease, or nutritional imbalance.
Abnormal amino acid profile suggests inborn errors of metabolism or malnutrition.
Low carnitine suggests metabolic disorders affecting fatty acid oxidation.
Abnormal organic acids indicate metabolic disorders such as mitochondrial dysfunction or amino acidopathies.
Abnormal levels suggest serotonin or dopamine metabolism disorders, linked to depression, anxiety, or schizophrenia.
Abnormal CAR indicates stress dysregulation, burnout, or adrenal dysfunction.

testName: Psychiatric Rating Scale – Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAM‑D)
resComment: High HAM‑D score indicates moderate to severe depression.

testName: Psychiatric Rating Scale – Beck Anxiety Inventory (BAI)
resComment: High BAI score indicates clinically significant anxiety.
Abnormal sleep study reveals sleep apnea, insomnia, or parasomnias.
Abnormal EEG may reveal psychiatric comorbidities such as epilepsy‑related mood disorders or encephalopathy.
Abnormal biopsy reveals inflammatory dermatoses, autoimmune disease, or malignancy such as melanoma.
Positive patch test indicates allergic contact dermatitis to specific allergens.
Abnormal dermoscopy reveals atypical pigment networks, vascular structures, or melanoma features.
Positive fungal scrapings indicate dermatophytosis, candidiasis, or other superficial fungal infections.
Positive immunofluorescence suggests autoimmune blistering diseases such as pemphigus or bullous pemphigoid.
Positive skin prick test indicates IgE-mediated allergy to environmental or food allergens.
Abnormal echocardiogram reveals valvular disease, cardiomyopathy, or reduced ejection fraction.
Abnormal cardiac MRI reveals myocardial infarction, fibrosis, congenital anomalies, or infiltrative disease.
Abnormal stress test suggests myocardial ischemia or reduced exercise tolerance.
Abnormal Holter reveals arrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia, or conduction blocks.
Abnormal angiography reveals coronary artery stenosis or occlusion, guiding revascularization.
Elevated NT-proBNP suggests heart failure and increased ventricular wall stress.
Abnormal spirometry suggests obstructive or restrictive lung disease such as asthma or COPD.
Abnormal chest CT reveals nodules, fibrosis, emphysema, or interstitial lung disease.
Abnormal bronchoscopy reveals airway obstruction, tumors, or infection. Allows biopsy and lavage.
Abnormal ABG indicates hypoxemia, hypercapnia, or acid-base imbalance due to respiratory failure.
Low DLCO suggests interstitial lung disease, emphysema, or pulmonary vascular disease.
Abnormal PEFR indicates airway obstruction, commonly used in asthma monitoring.
Abnormal EEG reveals epilepsy, encephalopathy, or focal brain lesions.
Abnormal EMG indicates neuromuscular disorders such as myopathy or neuropathy.
Abnormal NCS suggests peripheral neuropathy, demyelination, or axonal injury.
Abnormal brain MRI reveals stroke, tumors, demyelination, or neurodegenerative disease.
Abnormal CSF suggests meningitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage, or multiple sclerosis.
Abnormal evoked potentials indicate demyelinating disease, optic neuritis, or brainstem dysfunction.
Positive IgM indicates acute or recent typhoid infection. Appears early in the disease course.
Positive IgG indicates past typhoid infection or exposure. Suggests immune memory or chronic carrier state.
IgM+/IgG- suggests acute infection. IgM-/IgG+ suggests past infection or immunity. IgM+/IgG+ suggests ongoing infection transitioning to immunity.
Positive stool antigen confirms active H. pylori infection. Useful for diagnosis and post-treatment monitoring.
Positive breath test indicates active H. pylori infection. Highly sensitive and specific.
Positive IgG suggests past or chronic H. pylori infection. Not reliable for active infection.
Positive H. pylori Ab Test indicates recent or acute H. pylori infection.
Positive rapid antigen test provides quick evidence of H. pylori infection. Used for screening.
Positive TPHA indicates exposure to Treponema pallidum. Used as a confirmatory test for syphilis.
Reactive VDRL suggests active syphilis infection. May be false-positive in autoimmune or other infections.
Reactive RPR indicates syphilis infection. Used for screening and monitoring treatment response.
Positive FTA-ABS confirms syphilis infection. Highly specific treponemal test.
